narrative:
Representative Dan Newhouse is a third-generation Yakima Valley farmer who brought his agricultural expertise to Congress in 2015—and promptly used it to cultivate a very different kind of crop: corporate PAC donations. After a decade on both the House Agriculture Committee and the Appropriations Committee, Newhouse announced in December 2025 that he would not seek reelection, giving him the rare freedom of a lame duck who no longer needs to pretend he might change.

Newhouse occupies an unusual lane in Republican politics. He was one of only ten House Republicans to vote for Donald Trump's second impeachment after January 6th, a decision that earned him a Trump-backed primary challenger—and a survival story that made national headlines when he won anyway in both 2022 and 2024. That single act of conscience makes him look like a profile in courage, until you examine virtually everything else on his ledger.

As a member of both the Agriculture Committee and the Agriculture Appropriations Subcommittee, Newhouse is uniquely positioned to direct federal dollars to the industry he personally profits from as a farmer. His legislation focuses heavily on water rights for the Yakima Basin, specialty crop research funding, and agricultural appropriations that directly benefit Central Washington's farming operations—including, one might note, operations like his own. His campaign coffers have been generously fed by agricultural PACs, including a memorable $16,500 from Syngenta's PAC that he returned only after it became politically inconvenient, given that Syngenta is a subsidiary of a Chinese state-owned company. He positioned himself as a China hawk on agriculture while cashing checks from Chinese-controlled agricultural giants for years.

On the 80% Bill's twenty policies, Newhouse's record is a study in missed opportunities. He has not championed a congressional stock trading ban, has not pushed for Pentagon audit accountability despite sitting on Appropriations, and has shown no interest in term limits—though his retirement renders that last point moot. He has not co-sponsored the DISCLOSE Act or pushed to ban corporate PACs, the very entities that have funded his career. On paid family leave, right to repair, and junk fee bans, his record is similarly barren.

Newhouse will leave Congress in January 2027 having served twelve years—long enough to become exactly the kind of entrenched incumbent that term limits are designed to prevent, and short enough to claim he left voluntarily. His impeachment vote showed he has a spine. The question the 80% Bill asks is whether he ever considered using it for anything else.

Ralph Norman is a man who texted the White House chief of staff begging the President to invoke "Marshall Law" to overturn an election, and his only regret was the spelling. The South Carolina congressman and Freedom Caucus vice chair has built a political career on the premise that government is too big, too wasteful, and too corrupt — unless the government in question is keeping him in office or awarding contracts near his real estate developments.

Norman arrived in Washington as one of its wealthiest members, with assets estimated between $24.7 million and $95 million, largely in rental properties, mutual funds, and stocks accumulated through the Warren Norman Company, his family's commercial real estate empire. This is the man who now votes on housing policy, tax policy, and financial regulation. On the 80% Bill's proposal to ban corporate home buying, Norman has been conspicuously silent — perhaps because a real estate developer-turned-congressman opining on whether corporations should buy up housing stock would be the conflict of interest equivalent of a fox reviewing henhouse security protocols.

To his credit, Norman has been a genuine champion of term limits, proposing a constitutional amendment for three House terms and two Senate terms and declaring that limits would "cut the influence of special interest lobbyists." He scored a perfect 100% on the Heritage Action scorecard in the 117th Congress, which tells you everything about his priorities: slashing government programs that working families depend on while opposing any regulation of the corporations and wealthy donors who fund his campaigns.

Norman praised Elon Musk's DOGE efforts to gut the Social Security Administration, hinting that "we've got many more to come, like Social Security." He called Medicaid cuts in Trump's reconciliation bill "righting a wrong." For a man worth tens of millions in real estate, telling constituents that their Social Security and healthcare are luxuries the nation cannot afford takes a special kind of audacity.

On banning stock trading, dark money disclosure, Pentagon audits, Medicare drug negotiation, and paid family leave, Norman's record is a vast emptiness — the legislative equivalent of a vacant lot. He has shown no interest in the reforms that 80% of Americans support, preferring instead to focus on banning CBDCs and texting White House staff about overturning democracy. South Carolina's 5th District sent Norman to Congress to fight for them. Instead, he fights for the freedom of the already-free and the wealth of the already-wealthy.

Joe Neguse is the U.S. Representative for Colorado's 2nd Congressional District, a Democrat who has held the seat since 2019. The son of Eritrean refugees who fled a war-torn East African nation over forty years ago, Neguse became the first Eritrean-American elected to Congress and Colorado's first Black member of Congress [1]. A CU-Boulder law grad who once served as a regent of the University of Colorado, he now holds one of the most powerful perches in the House minority: Assistant Democratic Leader, the number-three position in his caucus [2]. He sits on the Judiciary, Natural Resources, and Rules committees, giving him a hand in everything from intellectual property law to federal lands policy [3].

On the 80% Bill scorecard, Neguse is one of the most aggressive reformers in the House. He has spent four years leading the charge to ban congressional stock trading, co-sponsoring the TRUST in Congress Act and signing the discharge petition to force a floor vote after Speaker Johnson buried the bill [4][5]. He introduced the Close the Revolving Door Act with Alexandria Ocasio-Cortez, imposing a lifetime lobbying ban on former members of Congress [6]. He is the lead House author of a constitutional amendment to overturn Citizens United, and has used the anniversary of that ruling to rally colleagues against dark money [7][8]. His "End Corruption Now" agenda, a seven-bill package launched with a cohort of next-generation Democrats, bundles stock trading bans, lobbying restrictions, and corporate board prohibitions into a single reform platform endorsed by CREW, Public Citizen, and the Project on Government Oversight [9]. He voted for the Inflation Reduction Act, which included Medicare drug price negotiation provisions and a 15% corporate minimum tax [10]. He helped pass the George Floyd Justice in Policing Act out of the House, which included federal body camera mandates [11]. He even led the introduction of the FARM Act, a right-to-repair bill requiring equipment manufacturers to give farmers access to parts, software, and documentation [12].

So where is the asterisk? Follow the money. Neguse's top donors include Brownstein Hyatt Farber Schreck, the powerhouse Denver-based lobbying and law firm that has represented the Colorado Oil and Gas Association in litigation against cities that tried to ban fracking [13][14]. In the 2024 cycle, OpenSecrets identified 79 donors to Neguse who both lobby and have former members as lobbyists -- a remarkable figure for someone whose signature bill would ban the revolving door [13]. His donor rolls also feature America's Credit Unions and the National Air Traffic Controllers Association, standard Democratic fare, but Brownstein's presence is hard to square with a man proposing lifetime lobbying bans [14]. The lobbying ban bill itself would put some of his own Colorado political network out of work, as the Colorado Sun noted [6].

Neguse is that rare creature: a reformer whose legislative portfolio genuinely mirrors what supermajorities of Americans want, from stock trading bans to Citizens United repeal to prescription drug negotiation. He has built an entire brand around anti-corruption, and unlike many colleagues, he has actually introduced the bills to back it up. But the Brownstein money is the pebble in the shoe -- the firm's lobbying arm touches every industry Neguse's reforms would constrain. The 80% Bill asks representatives to side with the people over the powerful. Neguse talks that talk more fluently than almost anyone in Congress. Whether his donor base agrees is another question entirely.

Zach Nunn represents Iowa's 3rd Congressional District, which covers Des Moines and the surrounding suburbs, a swing seat he flipped from Democrat Cindy Axne in 2022 by running as a young, military-tested conservative with a bipartisan streak [1]. An Air Force veteran who flew combat missions in Afghanistan and a former Iowa state legislator, Nunn arrived in Washington with the kind of resume that suggests someone who might actually buck his party on popular issues [2]. He serves on the Financial Services Committee and the Agriculture Committee, two panels with direct jurisdiction over several of the 80% Bill's core proposals — and two panels where industry lobbyists maintain permanent encampments [3].

On the twenty issues with supermajority public support, Nunn has carved out a few positions that let him claim independence without actually threatening anyone important. He has been vocal about protecting U.S. farmland from foreign purchases, introducing the Protecting American Farmland from Foreign Adversaries Act and making it a signature issue — a shrewd play in Iowa, where agriculture is identity and Chinese land acquisitions generate real anxiety [4]. He has supported congressional term limits and backed transparency around the Epstein files, positions that are popular, cost-free, and guaranteed never to produce uncomfortable votes [5]. He has also spoken about lowering prescription drug costs in general terms, though like his Iowa colleague Miller-Meeks, he voted against the Inflation Reduction Act that actually gave Medicare the power to negotiate [6].

The gaps in Nunn's record are revealing. Despite sitting on the Financial Services Committee — the panel with direct oversight of Wall Street, housing markets, and banking regulation — he has not cosponsored legislation to ban congressional stock trading, ban corporate purchases of single-family homes, or close the carried interest loophole [7]. On campaign finance reform, his record is bare: no support for banning corporate PACs, no cosponsorship of the DISCLOSE Act, and no interest in a constitutional amendment to overturn Citizens United. He has been silent on paid family leave, police body cameras, and auditing the Pentagon. The pattern is consistent: Nunn engages on issues where populist positioning helps him in a swing district, and disappears on issues where a vote might alienate the financial interests whose support he needs to hold a competitive seat.

Those financial interests have not been shy about investing. In his 2024 cycle, Nunn raised over $4 million, a substantial haul for a sophomore member [8]. His top donors include the securities and investment industry, real estate firms, and insurance companies — precisely the sectors regulated by his Financial Services Committee seat [9]. The American Bankers Association, the National Association of Realtors, and various hedge fund-affiliated PACs have all contributed, creating the kind of cozy arrangement where the congressman oversees the industries that fund his campaigns [10]. Defense contractors also feature among his donors, which aligns neatly with his silence on Pentagon accountability.

Nunn is a swing-district Republican who has figured out the formula: pick two or three populist issues that play well in Iowa (farmland, term limits, Epstein), talk about them constantly, and quietly shelve everything else on the list. His military service and youthful energy give him a reform-minded veneer, but his Financial Services Committee voting record and donor portfolio tell a more conventional story. In a district that flips back and forth between parties precisely because voters want someone who will challenge the establishment, Nunn offers the appearance of independence while delivering reliable service to the industries that sign his checks.

Jay Obernolte has represented California's 23rd Congressional District -- the vast, sparsely populated High Desert stretching from Big Bear Lake to Death Valley -- since 2021. A Republican with a genuinely unusual resume, Obernolte holds a master's degree in artificial intelligence from UCLA and is the founder and president of FarSight Studios, an independent video game company that counts Sony, Microsoft, and Apple among its clients [1][2]. He is the only sitting member of Congress with a graduate degree in AI, which earned him the co-chairmanship of the House Bipartisan Task Force on Artificial Intelligence alongside Democrat Ted Lieu [3]. He also serves on the Energy and Commerce Committee and chairs the Science, Space, and Technology Subcommittee on Research and Technology [4]. Before Congress, he was mayor of Big Bear Lake and a California state assemblyman. His estimated net worth is between $1 million and $5 million [5].

On the 80% Bill's twenty planks, Obernolte's record is a study in selective populism. His district is home to five military installations -- Fort Irwin, Edwards Air Force Base, Naval Air Weapons Station China Lake, Marine Corps Logistics Base Barstow, and Twentynine Palms -- and he has secured tens of millions in NDAA funding for them, including $45 million for a wastewater treatment plant at Twentynine Palms and $52 million for a simulation center at Fort Irwin [6][7]. Yet he has shown no interest in auditing the Pentagon that writes those checks, nor in repealing the AUMFs that keep the military on permanent footing. He voted against the Inflation Reduction Act, which contained Medicare drug price negotiation provisions, calling it a bill that "will not actually reduce inflation" [8]. He opposed the Build Back Better Act, which included paid family leave and expanded social spending, citing deficit concerns [9]. When pressed on whether the wealthy pay their fair share, he responded that the top one percent already pay "nearly half of all federal taxes" and asked critics to define what "fair share" even means -- a rhetorical dodge that doubles as a policy position against the wealth taxes and carried interest reforms in the 80% Bill [10]. He has introduced bipartisan legislation on vehicle right-to-repair, but only in the narrow context of ensuring car modifications don't compromise safety systems -- a far cry from the broad right-to-repair movement [11].

To his credit, Obernolte has occasionally crossed party lines in ways that suggest independence. He introduced the Election Results Accountability Act, requiring states to count 90% of ballots within 72 hours and certify results within two weeks -- a transparency measure that, while framed as election integrity, aligns with the spirit of fair elections [12]. His AI work has been genuinely bipartisan, and his legislative output on cybersecurity and remote military base staffing reflects real constituent service rather than donor service [3][7]. But on the core economic populism that defines the 80% Bill -- taxing the ultra-wealthy, closing the carried interest loophole, banning corporate PACs, funding Social Security, banning junk fees -- Obernolte is either silent or opposed.

Follow the money and the silence makes sense. Obernolte received contributions from lobbyists tracked by OpenSecrets, and his campaign finance profile reflects the standard Republican ecosystem of defense, real estate, and finance industry support [13][14]. He champions tax cut extensions, proposing a tiered capital gains system with lower rates for assets held longer -- a structure that overwhelmingly benefits the investor class, not the High Desert constituents living in one of California's most affordable (and economically struggling) regions [10]. His district may be full of military families who would benefit from paid family leave and funded Social Security, but his voting record suggests their representative is more attentive to the tax preferences of asset holders than to the kitchen-table economics of Barstow and Victorville.

Jay Obernolte is a smart man in Congress -- literally the only one with an AI degree -- and his tech fluency is a genuine asset to the institution. But intelligence is not the same as alignment with what 80% of Americans want. On stock trading bans, dark money disclosure, wealth taxes, Pentagon accountability, and corporate PAC reform, Obernolte is either absent or on the wrong side of the consensus. The 80% Bill asks whether representatives will fight for the overwhelmingly popular. Obernolte would rather talk about robots.

Burgess Owens is a man who filed for bankruptcy five times, had $1.5 million in personal debts discharged by the courts, and then got elected to Congress where he chaired a hearing railing against student loan forgiveness because "debt cannot be canceled." Except, apparently, when it is his debt, in which case the courts were very helpful. The former NFL safety who won Super Bowl XV with the Raiders brought the same ball-handling skills to campaign finance: in 2020, his campaign accepted $135,500 in contributions that exceeded federal limits, prompting an FEC investigation and a nearly $4,000 fine for failing to timely report $34,000 in additional contributions. His campaign blamed the mix-up on having "over 50,000 donors" making electronic contributions, which is the political equivalent of blaming autocorrect.

From his seat on the Education and Workforce Committee, Owens has positioned himself as a culture warrior rather than a policy wonk. He opposed the For the People Act, which would have expanded voting rights and campaign finance transparency. He testified against reparations. He told struggling student borrowers to "get a second job" — advice that hits differently coming from a man whose debts were wiped clean by bankruptcy courts. On January 6, 2021, he voted to certify Arizona's electors but objected to Pennsylvania's, threading the insurrection needle with the precision of a man who wanted to please everyone and satisfied no one.

On the 80% Bill's twenty policies, Owens is a near-perfect zero. He has taken no public position on banning congressional stock trading, ending dark money, imposing term limits, banning corporate PACs, or passing the DISCLOSE Act. He has not supported Medicare drug price negotiation, paid family leave, junk fee bans, or right-to-repair legislation. The man who had $1.5 million forgiven has nothing to say about taxing the ultra-wealthy or closing corporate tax loopholes.

In March 2026, Owens announced he would not seek reelection, citing a "next mission" best completed outside of office. Utah's new congressional map, which created a heavily Democratic district, may have helped clarify that mission. He leaves Congress having accomplished the remarkable feat of turning personal financial catastrophe into a platform for lecturing others about fiscal responsibility — and collecting a government salary along the way.

Alexandria Ocasio-Cortez is the rare member of Congress who actually aligns with the 80% Bill platform — which is precisely what makes her so dangerous to the institution. While her colleagues treat popular reform as a rhetorical garnish for fundraising emails, AOC has the inconvenient habit of introducing legislation that matches her talking points. Terrifying.

On stock trading, she co-authored the bipartisan ETHICS Act alongside Rep. Josh Riley, Rep. Michael Cloud (R-TX), and others, which would ban members of Congress, their spouses, and dependents from trading individual stocks. When House GOP leadership floated a watered-down alternative in January 2026, she called it out as insufficient: "Any bill that still allows Members of Congress to own and trade stocks falls far short of what the American people want and deserve." A politician who reads the bill before commenting on it — revolutionary concept.

Her campaign finance record is almost comically clean by congressional standards. In the 2024 cycle, just 0.3% of her funding came from PACs, while nearly 70% came from small donors giving under $200. She raised $9.6 million in Q1 2025 alone — all without a single corporate PAC dollar. Her top contributor was Google employees at $35,864, which in congressional terms is what falls between the couch cushions. She has pledged to reject corporate PAC money entirely, and unlike most of her colleagues, the FEC receipts actually back it up.

She serves on the House Energy and Commerce Committee and is a member of the Medicare for All Caucus, the Congressional Progressive Caucus, and roughly every caucus that includes the word "equality" in its name. She championed the Green New Deal with Senator Ed Markey and famously suggested marginal tax rates of 60-70% on the ultra-wealthy — a proposal that polled at over 59% approval before the pundit class informed Americans they were supposed to hate it.

On the Epstein files, AOC secured votes for full release and excoriated the DOJ when Attorney General Pam Bondi oversaw a dump of heavily redacted documents. "Now the coverup is out in the open," she declared, demanding Bondi resign. She pushed for the DISCLOSE Act and has called for overturning Citizens United since her first campaign.

Her financial disclosure shows assets between $3,000 and $45,000 with student loan debt — making her one of the least wealthy members of Congress. She wore a "Tax the Rich" dress to the Met Gala, which outraged people who apparently believe the rich should only be taxed in settings where they are not present. The woman who once worked as a bartender now outraises every House colleague. The establishment finds this deeply upsetting.

Ilhan Omar's biography reads like the kind of story America tells itself at its best: a Somali refugee who fled civil war at eight, spent four years in a Kenyan refugee camp, arrived in the United States at twelve speaking no English, and within two decades was sworn into Congress as the first African refugee, the first woman of color from Minnesota, and one of the first two Muslim women ever to serve in the House [1]. Representing Minneapolis's 5th District — the most Democratic seat in the Upper Midwest with a Cook PVI of D+26 — Omar has held the position since 2019, winning re-election by margins that suggest her constituents find the perpetual controversy surrounding her considerably less alarming than her critics do [2].

As Deputy Chair of the Congressional Progressive Caucus, Omar has staked out positions on the 80% Bill's priorities that range from enthusiastic to maximalist. Her Homes for All Act proposed a trillion-dollar investment to build twelve million new public and affordable housing units, operating under the apparently radical theory that people should have roofs over their heads [3]. She has pushed for tuition-free college, cancellation of student loan debt, a fifteen-dollar minimum wage, universal healthcare, and the abolition of ICE — a policy portfolio that makes her either a visionary or a cautionary tale, depending on which cable network you happen to be watching. Her advocacy contributed to the Biden administration's cancellation of nearly $144 billion in student debt for four million borrowers, a figure that congressional moderates nervously pretend had nothing to do with progressive pressure [4].

Omar's tenure has been defined as much by the attacks against her as by the policies she advances. In September 2025, Republicans brought a censure resolution seeking to strip her from the Education and Workforce and Budget committees — an effort that failed by a single vote when four Republicans broke ranks [5]. In January 2026, a man rushed the stage at her Minneapolis town hall and sprayed her with an unknown substance, an assault the FBI took over investigating amid what Omar described as threats that "skyrocket" whenever the political temperature rises [6]. That she resumed speaking at the event after being attacked tells you something about either her composure or the frequency with which she has had to develop it.

On foreign policy, Omar remains one of Congress's most outspoken critics of Israel's military campaigns and settlement policies, a position that has earned her censure attempts, removal from the Foreign Affairs Committee in the previous Congress, and a level of bipartisan hostility typically reserved for members who commit actual crimes rather than express unpopular opinions [7]. She supports the BDS movement and has consistently challenged the influence of pro-Israel lobbying in American politics, positions her supporters consider principled and her detractors consider disqualifying — a divide that maps neatly onto whether one believes congressional speech should have boundaries beyond the ones already set by law.

Whatever one makes of her politics, Omar's legislative ambitions on housing, healthcare, education, and labor align squarely with the planks that poll at seventy or eighty percent support among American voters. The irony of her career is that a refugee who became a citizen at seventeen may understand the promise of American democracy more literally than many of her colleagues who were born into it — and her insistence on holding the country to that promise is precisely what makes her so polarizing [8].

Jon Ossoff is the junior senator from Georgia, elected in the January 2021 runoff that handed Democrats control of the Senate. At thirty-three he became the youngest sitting senator, and before that he spent eight years as CEO of Insight TWI, a London-based investigative documentary company whose films exposed corruption, war crimes, and human trafficking across three continents [1]. He now chairs the Permanent Subcommittee on Investigations, the Senate's oldest and most powerful investigative body, armed with subpoena power and a mandate to root out government corruption [2]. He also sits on the Judiciary, Banking, Homeland Security, Appropriations, Intelligence, and Rules committees. If the resume reads like someone who genuinely wants to clean house, that is very much the brand.

On the 80% Bill scorecard, Ossoff is one of the more active players in Congress. He is the lead sponsor of the Ban Congressional Stock Trading Act, which in 2024 became the first stock-trading ban ever to pass a Senate committee [3]. He placed his own portfolio in a blind trust upon taking office [4]. He introduced the Ban Corporate PACs Act with Senator Mark Kelly to prohibit for-profit corporations from bankrolling political campaigns [5]. He has called Citizens United the catalyst for "corruption on steroids" and backed efforts to require donor disclosure [6]. He voted to repeal the 1991 and 2002 Iraq AUMFs, calling them "outdated" authorities that "undermine Congress's constitutional role in matters of war and peace" [7]. And he championed the $35 insulin price cap for seniors that became law through the Inflation Reduction Act [8].

Where it gets interesting is the money. Ossoff refuses corporate PAC dollars and will tell anyone who listens that billionaire influence is poisoning democracy. Yet FEC filings show his campaigns have collected contributions from more than seventy billionaires since 2017, including members of the Soros family, former Google CEO Eric Schmidt, LinkedIn co-founder Reid Hoffman, and Cox Enterprises heir James Cox-Chambers [9]. His top employer-linked individual donors read like a Silicon Valley roll call: nearly $953,000 from people at Alphabet, $296,000 from Apple, $276,000 from Microsoft, $255,000 from Amazon, and $225,000 from Meta [10]. More than eighty percent of his most recent fundraising haul came from outside Georgia, with over half of maxed-out donors hailing from California, New York, or the D.C.-Maryland-Virginia corridor [9]. Technically none of that is corporate PAC money, which is true in the way that a loophole is technically not a hole.

The contradiction is real but not quite damning. Ossoff can fairly argue that individual donations from employees at tech companies are not the same as a Lockheed Martin PAC writing a check, and that banning corporate PACs while accepting individual contributions is a coherent, if conveniently comfortable, position. He has put legislative skin in the game on stock trading, corporate PACs, and donor transparency in ways most of his colleagues have not. But a senator who rails against concentrated wealth while cashing checks from seventy-plus billionaires is, at minimum, performing a high-wire act that deserves close watching — especially as he faces a tough 2026 reelection in a state where his rhetoric about "the Epstein class" of elites [11] lands differently depending on who is reading the donor rolls.

Andrew Ogles is what happens when you let a man who lied on his resume write laws for 700,000 people. The congressman from Tennessee's 5th District campaigned as an "economist and tax policy expert," which sounds impressive until you learn his actual college major was Liberal Studies, he took exactly one economics course at a community college (he got a C), and his claimed graduate work at Vanderbilt and Dartmouth consisted of non-credit online certificate programs. He also claimed to be a trained police officer and an expert in international sex crimes. None of it was true.

But the resume fiction is just the opening act. The FBI executed a search warrant on Ogles in August 2024, seizing his phone as part of a federal criminal investigation into campaign finance fraud. The core allegation: Ogles initially reported loaning his 2022 campaign $320,000, then quietly amended the figure down to $20,000 — a $300,000 discrepancy he never adequately explained, likely because he never had the money in the first place. The House Ethics Committee opened an investigation that carried into the 119th Congress. Career prosecutors later withdrew from the case after Trump's inauguration, with the matter transferred to DOJ headquarters in Washington — a move that raised eyebrows about political interference in the investigation.

On policy, Ogles is a Freedom Caucus hardliner who votes against everything and proposes nothing that would help his constituents. He was one of the original 19 to vote against Kevin McCarthy for Speaker, voted against disaster relief citing the deficit, and opposed the Fiscal Responsibility Act. He has not co-sponsored legislation on any of the 80% Bill's 20 planks — no stock trading ban, no dark money disclosure, no junk fee protections, no paid family leave.

Instead, Ogles spends his time on culture war provocations. In March 2026, he posted that "Muslims don't belong in American society" and proposed legislation to ban immigration from Muslim-majority countries. He has called for Christian nationalism and opposes both abortion rights and same-sex marriage.

The 5th District deserves a representative whose qualifications are real and whose campaign funds are accounted for. By every measure the 80% Bill tracks — transparency, anti-corruption, working-family policy — Ogles scores a zero, which may be the only honest number associated with his career.
